graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to fully trace obligations for (a) a medical diagnostic AI system specifically “as a product within medical device legislation,” and (b) the deployer’s duties in a hospital/public health setting, because the excerpted pages here only cover parts of the general framework (value chain concepts, conformity/presumption of conformity, AI Office powers, documentation/information characteristics, and some GPAI-provider points), not the medical-device interface or deployer obligations.

From the context available, the compliance chain and interactions are:

- GPAI model provider (general-purpose AI model)
  - Must comply with “obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models” under the Regulation (the context states such obligations are imposed and that providers are subject to the Regulation’s obligations).
  - Should rely on “codes of practice” and/or “European harmonised standards” to demonstrate compliance; compliance with a European harmonised standard “should grant providers the presumption of conformity.” If codes/standards aren’t available or the provider chooses not to rely on them, the provider can “demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means.”
  - Must increase transparency on “data used in pre-training and training of general-purpose AI models.”
  - Must comply with Union copyright law/standards (including needing authorisation from rightholders where required), especially in connection with text and data mining.
  - Is subject to AI Office monitoring/investigation powers: the AI Office can request documentation/information, conduct evaluations (using independent expertise), and request measures from GPAI model providers.

- AI system provider (integrating the GPAI model into the final medical diagnostic AI system / final product)
  - Along the AI value chain, multiple parties can supply components/processes used for training/testing/integration; the “provider” of the final AI system has the obligation to ensure the AI system “embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation.”
  - Conformity assessment is carried out by the provider; providers “demonstrate conformity with requirements of this Regulation.”
  - Documentation/information duties exist to support compliance assessment and post‑market monitoring: information should be “machine-readable,” “understandable,” and include capabilities, limitations, training, type of decisions, testing; documentation should contain accessible/comprehensive/meaningful/understandable information.
  - Providers should have a post‑market monitoring system and are required to have a “system to report serious incidents.”

- Deployer (hospital using the AI system)
  - The context only states that “deployers use AI systems” and that deployers “complement obligations of the provider.” It also indicates natural persons should be notified of interaction/exposure to AI systems (a transparency/notification concept), but it doesn’t specify the deployer’s concrete duties in a hospital/public health setting.

- How these interact (value-chain interaction)
  - The final AI-system provider remains responsible for the AI-system’s compliance when embedded in the final product, even though upstream parties supplied models/components/processes.
  - Upstream GPAI compliance support mechanisms (codes of practice, harmonised standards, or alternative adequate means) can feed into downstream compliance evidence, but they do not replace the downstream provider’s conformity assessment and product compliance duties.
  - Oversight can occur at multiple points: the AI Office can directly monitor/investigate GPAI model providers, and market surveillance/competent authorities can evaluate AI systems (the context mentions market surveillance authorities evaluating and cooperating with the AI Office).

If you share the relevant EU AI Act excerpts on (1) the medical devices/high‑risk interface and (2) deployer obligations (hospital/public health), I can produce a complete step-by-step compliance chain for each actor and the handoffs (documentation, responsibilities, monitoring, incident reporting).
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: A company builds a general-purpose AI model, integrates it into a medical diagnostic AI system, and deploys it in a hospital. Trace the compliance chain: What obligations apply to the GPAI model provider, the AI system provider as a product within medical device legislation, the deployer in a public health setting, and how do these interact?

I don’t have enough information in the provided context to fully trace obligations for (a) a medical diagnostic AI system specifically “as a product within medical device legislation,” and (b) the deployer’s duties in a hospital/public health setting, because the excerpted pages here only cover parts of the general framework (value chain concepts, conformity/presumption of conformity, AI Office powers, documentation/information characteristics, and some GPAI-provider points), not the medical-device interface or deployer obligations.

From the context available, the compliance chain and interactions are:

- GPAI model provider (general-purpose AI model)
  - Must comply with “obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models” under the Regulation (the context states such obligations are imposed and that providers are subject to the Regulation’s obligations).

  - Must increase transparency on “data used in pre-training and training of general-purpose AI models.”

  - Conformity assessment is carried out by the provider; providers “demonstrate conformity with requirements of this Regulation.”
  - Documentation/information duties exist to support compliance assessment and post‑market monitoring: information should be “machine-readable,” “understandable,” and include capabilities, limitations, training, type of decisions, testing; documentation should contain accessible/comprehensive/meaningful/understandable information.
  - Providers should have a post‑market monitoring system and are required to have a “system to report serious incidents.”

- Deployer (hospital using the AI system)
  - The context only states that “deployers use AI systems” and that deployers “complement obligations of the provider.” It also indicates natural persons should be notified of interaction/exposure to AI systems (a transparency/notification concept), but it doesn’t specify the deployer’s concrete duties in a hospital/public health setting.

- How these interact (value-chain interaction)
  - The final AI-system provider remains responsible for the AI-system’s compliance when embedded in the final product, even though upstream parties supplied models/components/processes.
  - Upstream GPAI compliance support mechanisms (codes of practice, harmonised standards, or alternative adequate means) can feed into downstream compliance evidence, but they do not replace the downstream provider’s conformity assessment and product compliance duties.
  - Oversight can occur at multiple points: the AI Office can directly monitor/investigate GPAI model providers, and market surveillance/competent authorities can evaluate AI systems (the context mentions market surveillance authorities evaluating and cooperating with the AI Office).

If you share the relevant EU AI Act excerpts on (1) the medical devices/high‑risk interface and (2) deployer obligations (hospital/public health), I can produce a complete step-by-step compliance chain for each actor and the handoffs (documentation, responsibilities, monitoring, incident reporting).

Content: Text Content:
published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European  harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI  models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice  or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant  market actors that are placing them
----
 obligations of the provider established in this Regulation and should, in particular, ensure that the AI system  embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation. (88) Along the AI value chain multiple parties often supply AI systems, tools and services but also components or  processes that are incorporated by the provider into the AI system with various objectives, including the model  training, model retraining, model testing and evaluation, integration into software, or other aspects of model  development. Those
----
 should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and  compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI  Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this  Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by  requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make  use of independent expertise, the
----
 considered to be the provider for the purposes of this Regulation,  and when that provider has not expressly excluded the change of the AI system into a high-risk AI system, the  former provider should nonetheless closely cooperate and make available the necessary information and provide the  reasonably expected technical access and other assistance that are required for the fulfilment of the obligations set  out in this Regulation, in particular regarding the compliance with the conformity assessment of high-risk AI  systems. (87) In addition,

chunks.text: obligations of the provider established in this Regulation and should, in particular, ensure that the AI system embedded in the final product complies with the requirements of this Regulation. (88) Along the AI value chain multiple parties often supply AI systems, tools and services but also components or processes that are incorporated by the provider into the AI system with various objectives, including the model training, model retraining, model testing and evaluation, integration into software, or other aspects of model development. Those

published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant market actors that are placing them

should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make use of independent expertise, the

considered to be the provider for the purposes of this Regulation, and when that provider has not expressly excluded the change of the AI system into a high-risk AI system, the former provider should nonetheless closely cooperate and make available the necessary information and provide the reasonably expected technical access and other assistance that are required for the fulfilment of the obligations set out in this Regulation, in particular regarding the compliance with the conformity assessment of high-risk AI systems.
